title: Dr. Rezwana Karim Snigdha — Feminist Scholar, Bangladesh
description: Dr. Rezwana Karim Snigdha is one of Bangladesh's leading feminist scholars and public intellectuals. A professor in the Anthropology Department at Jahangirnagar University, her scholarship explores gender, sexuality, embodiment, social inequality, climate vulnerability, and the lived experiences of marginalized communities across South Asia. Through ethnographic research, teaching, and public engagement, she contributes to advancing feminist knowledge, social justice, and inclusive development.
heroRole: Professor of Anthropology, Jahangirnagar University · PhD, Auckland University of Technology, New Zealand.
grant: World Bank / UGC HEAT Grant — USD 80,000 · 2025 · Top 10.2% Nationally
aboutQuote: Ethnographic explorations at the threshold of gender, body, and sexuality.
about: Dr. Rezwana Karim Snigdha is a feminist scholar, gender expert, and public intellectual whose work contributes to critical conversations on gender, sexuality, embodiment, and social justice in Bangladesh and beyond.

With over fifteen years of experience in teaching, ethnographic research, training, and advocacy, she is currently a Professor of Anthropology at Jahangirnagar University. She earned her PhD in Social Sciences from Auckland University of Technology (AUT), New Zealand, in 2021. Her doctoral research, Beyond Binaries: An Ethnographic Study of Hijra in Dhaka, Bangladesh, offers a nuanced and ethnographically grounded exploration of gender diversity, identity, and belonging in South Asia.

While widely recognized for her pioneering work on gender and sexuality, her scholarship extends across gender justice, body politics, reproductive health, medical anthropology, sociology of health, climate vulnerability, and the everyday experiences of marginalized populations.

Dr. Snigdha is actively engaged with several national and international academic, research, and policy networks, including the Sex Tech Lab at The New School, New York; the World Anthropology Union (WAU); the International Union of Anthropological and Ethnological Sciences (IUAES); the Canada–Bangladesh Social Justice & Research Alliance; the Duke of Edinburgh’s Award Foundation; and the Kapaeeng Foundation. She also serves on the Trustee Board of the National Child Welfare Trust and the Research Committee of the National Youth Development Institute, reflecting her commitment to connecting scholarship with public service and social transformation.

Her academic engagement also includes serving as an international reviewer for leading journals, including the Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute (JRAI), Asian Women, the Journal of Homosexuality, and Contemporary South Asia.

Beyond academia, Dr. Snigdha is a prominent public voice on gender, democracy, culture, and social change. As a frequent television commentator, keynote speaker, and public intellectual, she brings anthropological perspectives to wider audiences, connecting rigorous scholarship with public debate, policy engagement, and advocacy for a more inclusive and equitable society.
bioLong: Dr. Rezwana Karim Snigdha is an anthropologist, feminist scholar, gender expert, and interdisciplinary researcher whose work critically engages with gender, sexuality, embodiment, and social justice in South Asia. With over fifteen years of experience in teaching, ethnographic research, training, and advocacy, she grounds her scholarship in ethnographic inquiry and decolonial feminist praxis. Her work examines the lived realities of marginalized communities and the social, cultural, and political structures through which identity, inequality, and exclusion are produced and negotiated.

She completed her BSS (2006) and MSS (2007) in anthropology at Jahangirnagar University, securing first class, first position, in both degrees, before earning her PhD in social sciences from Auckland University of Technology (AUT), New Zealand, in 2021. Her doctoral research, Beyond Binaries: An Ethnographic Study of Hijra in Dhaka, Bangladesh, offers a nuanced and ethnographically grounded exploration of gender diversity and the complex negotiations of identity, recognition, and social life among Hijra communities in Bangladesh.

Her research lies at the intersections of gender, body, sexuality, health, and social inequality. Her areas of scholarship encompass gender justice and body politics, Hijra and queer studies, masculinity studies, medical anthropology, sexual and reproductive health, sociology of health, climate vulnerability among coastal women, and the politics of religion and media. Across these interconnected areas, her work explores how structural inequalities, cultural norms, institutional practices, and everyday negotiations shape people’s experiences and agency. Her 2023 ethnography, In Search of Women’s Self (নারীসত্তার অন্বেষণে), reflects her sustained scholarly engagement with women’s lived experiences, identity, and agency with over 320+ citations.

Dr. Snigdha has presented her research at the World Anthropology Union (WAU) and the International Union of Anthropological and Ethnological Sciences (IUAES) World Congresses and has delivered lectures at Jawaharlal Nehru University, South Asian University, and the University of Iowa. In 2025, she was awarded a competitive World Bank–UGC HEAT research grant of USD 80,000, with her proposal ranking among the top 10.2% of submissions nationally.

Her academic engagement extends across several national and international research and policy networks, including the Sex Tech Lab at The New School, New York; the Canada–Bangladesh Social Justice & Research Alliance; the Duke of Edinburgh’s Award Foundation; and the Kapaeeng Foundation.

Alongside her research and teaching, Dr. Snigdha holds a range of academic leadership, institutional service, and professional roles. She serves as Hall Provost of Begum Sufia Kamal Hall, a trustee of the National Child Welfare Trust, an international coordinator for IMRF Bangladesh, and a member of the Research Committee of the National Youth Development Institute. She also contributes to international academic publishing as a peer reviewer for the Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute (JRAI), Asian Women, the Journal of Homosexuality, and Contemporary South Asia.

Beyond her academic and institutional roles, Dr. Snigdha is an active public voice on gender, democracy, culture, and social change. As a television commentator, keynote speaker, and public intellectual, she brings anthropological perspectives into wider public conversations, connecting scholarly research with public debate, policy engagement, and advocacy for a more inclusive and equitable society.
